reasoning_model_1: The model generates the text 'Don't worry, everything will be fine' with perfect content accuracy and natural prosody (00:00-00:03). Regarding the core requirement 'low pitch', the model succeeds admirably. The voice starts in a lower register on 'Don't worry' (00:00-00:01) and descends even further during 'everything will be fine' (00:01-00:03). The timbre is deep and resonant, creating a reassuring atmosphere that fully aligns with the instruction to use a low pitch. There are no artifacts or fluency issues.
reasoning_model_2: The model correctly transcribes the text content 'Don't worry, everything will be fine' (00:00-00:02). However, regarding the core paralinguistic feature 'Pitch', the model fails completely. The user requested a 'low pitch', but the generated audio features a very high-pitched, breathless voice profile. Throughout the entire clip, particularly on the vowels in 'worry' (00:00) and 'fine' (00:02), the fundamental frequency is elevated. This expresses the exact opposite of the target characteristic.
model_comparison: Model 1 (T1) is the undeniable winner in this evaluation. The task required a specific control over the 'Pitch' dimension—specifically requesting a 'low pitch'. T1 delivered a deep, resonant voice that perfectly matched this description. Model 2 (T2), conversely, generated a high-pitched voice. Since T2 expressed the opposite of the requested feature, it failed the core task constraint, whereas T1 executed it with high quality and naturalness.
